summary: The q2 dataset contains 10 problems (q1 through q10), each recording exactly 100 issues, indicating uniform activity with no single problem standing out by issue volume. The provided context lacks granular metadata such as issue status (open/resolved), timestamps, or content categories, so traction and solvability cannot be reliably differentiated at this time. Reliable prioritization will require enriched issue-level data beyond raw counts.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues each, suggesting either a placeholder/default count or a data export limitation rather than genuine differentiated activity.
No open-vs-resolved breakdown is available, so it is impossible to assess true proof status or which problems are nearing completion.
Absence of issue timestamps prevents identifying recent activity spikes or stale problems.
No categorization (e.g., logical gaps, notation errors, missing lemmas) is provided, blocking pattern analysis of common issue types.
The uniform distribution makes it impossible to identify which problems have genuine traction versus inflated or artifact counts.
highlights: Complete coverage: every problem in the dataset has associated issue tracking, ensuring no problem is unmonitored.
Consistent data structure across all 10 problems enables straightforward future automated analysis once richer metadata is added.
High issue counts overall indicate active engagement and thorough review of the proofs.

suggested_todos.title: Enrich issue records with status fields (open/resolved/in-review) to enable solvability assessment
Add issue categorization tags (e.g., missing lemma, notation, logical gap) to surface common patterns
Include timestamps on issues to identify recent activity and stale problems
Investigate why all problems report exactly 100 issues to rule out a data export or truncation artifact
Once enriched data is available, rank problems by ratio of resolved-to-open issues to flag near-complete proofs
